comment: Credential-store + shell-history reads that the June-2026 Miasma / Shai-Hulud agent-credential-theft worms perform. Complements credential-exfil.json by covering stores it missed (kube/gcloud/vault/docker/.git-credentials), non-PowerShell shell histories, secret-directory archiving, agent CLI auth files, and GPG/pass/wallet stores. Reader set is deliberately broad (findstr/Select-String/Copy-Item/rg/grep/robocopy), since the harvester does not care which binary reads the file.

rules.name: cloud/cluster/registry credential store read
rules.description: Reading a Git credential store, Kubernetes kubeconfig, gcloud credentials, a Vault token, or a Docker registry config. Each grants programmatic access to source control, clusters, cloud, or registries — exactly the stores the Miasma credential harvester sweeps.
rules.guidance:
1. Confirm the harness had a legitimate reason (git push, kubectl, cloud deploy, docker login).
2. If unexpected, rotate the affected credential: regenerate GitHub/GitLab tokens, re-issue the kubeconfig, rotate the Vault token, and `docker logout` then re-auth.
3. Review the relevant audit log for unexpected use in the last 24h.

rules.name: shell history credential mining
rules.description: Reading a shell's saved command history (bash/zsh/fish/python/psql/mysql). Developers routinely paste API keys, tokens, and passwords straight into a prompt, leaving them in plaintext in the history file — a primary Miasma harvest target. (PowerShell's ConsoleHost_history.txt is covered by cred-016.)
rules.guidance:
1. If unexpected, review the named history file for credentials you may have typed.
2. Clear it (e.g. `: > ~/.bash_history`) and rotate any token/password that appears in it.
3. Avoid pasting secrets into interactive shells while an agent is running.

rules.name: secret directory enumeration or archive
rules.description: Recursively listing or archiving a secret directory (.ssh, .aws, .gnupg, .kube, gcloud). Tarring or zipping ~/.ssh in one shot is the classic precursor to bulk credential exfiltration — it stages every key/token for a single upload.
rules.guidance:
1. A recursive listing or archive of a secret directory is rarely a legitimate coding step — confirm with the task.
2. If unexpected, treat every credential in that directory as exposed and rotate SSH keys, cloud keys, and GPG keys.
3. Check for a recently created archive and an outbound upload.

rules.name: agent / CLI auth token read
rules.description: Reading the stored auth tokens of a developer CLI or AI agent: Claude Code credentials, the GitHub CLI host token, the OpenAI/Codex auth file. These are long-lived tokens that grant access to the user's AI and developer accounts — and a worm that hijacks an agent can pivot through them.
rules.guidance:
1. Confirm the harness had a reason to read its own / a sibling CLI's auth file.
2. If unexpected, sign out and back in to rotate the token (e.g. `gh auth refresh`, re-login to the agent).
3. Review recent account activity for the affected service.

rules.name: decoy credential sentinel observed
rules.description: A Foreman decoy (canary) credential's sentinel token appeared in a command line. Nothing legitimate ever handles a decoy you planted as bait, so this means a process read one of the fake credential files and is now staging or exfiltrating its value — the highest-confidence sign of an active credential harvester.
rules.guidance: Treat this as an active credential-theft incident. Identify the process handling the decoy and what it did with it. Real credentials adjacent to the decoy paths should be considered targeted: rotate them. Preserve the event for investigation.

rules.name: GPG keyring / pass / crypto wallet read
rules.description: Reading a GPG private keyring, a `pass` password-store entry, or a cryptocurrency wallet file. These hold signing keys, stored passwords, and wallet seeds — high-value targets the Miasma payload enumerates alongside cloud credentials. (Password-manager databases like .kdbx are covered by cred-012.)
rules.guidance:
1. GPG secret-key, pass-store, or wallet reads are almost never a legitimate coding task — confirm immediately.
2. If unexpected, assume the secrets are exposed: rotate GPG keys, change pass-store entries, and move crypto funds to a fresh wallet.
